metadata.summary: GitHub Actions automation: pin actions to commit SHA, minimize GITHUB_TOKEN permissions, use OIDC for cloud auth.

content.title: GitHub Actions CI/CD
content.summary: GitHub Actions automates workflows defined in `.github/workflows/` YAML files, triggered by push, pull_request, schedule, and other events. The critical security rule: pin every third-party action to a full commit SHA, not a mutable tag. Always set explicit `permissions:` blocks at workflow or job level, defaulting to `contents: read`.

why.p: GitHub Actions is the native CI/CD for GitHub-hosted projects, eliminating webhook setup and credential management between CI and SCM. OIDC authentication (id-token: write) lets workflows authenticate to AWS/GCP/Azure without any stored long-lived secrets. Reusable workflows and composite actions eliminate copy-paste across repositories.

list.item: Project needs matrix builds across OS/versions/runtimes
Team wants OIDC-based credentialless cloud authentication
Open source project needs free CI minutes
Workflows must trigger on GitHub-native events (PR, release, issue)
Repository is on GitLab — use GitLab CI/CD instead
Deployment is GitOps/ArgoCD-managed — trigger ArgoCD sync rather than kubectl in Actions
Highly regulated environment requires runners on-prem and GitHub.com is not approved — self-hosted runners add operational burden; evaluate Jenkins or GitLab self-managed
`pull_request_target` needed with untrusted fork code — high injection risk; use carefully

rule.statement: Pin every third-party action to a full 40-character commit SHA, never a mutable tag like @v4 or @main.
rule.rationale: Tags are mutable — an action author or attacker who compromises the account can push malicious code to a tag. SHA references are immutable and cannot be changed after the fact.

example.title: Mutable action references
example.code: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 # mutable tag - uses: some-action/deploy@main # branch reference
example.note: Both can be replaced by the action publisher at any time.

example.title: Pinned action with SHA
example.note: Add a comment with the tag name for readability. Use Dependabot for automatic SHA updates.

rule.statement: Set permissions: at workflow or job level with only the scopes actually needed, defaulting contents: read.
rule.rationale: The implicit default grants read access to all scopes and write to some (contents). Explicit minimal permissions limit blast radius if a malicious action runs.

example.title: Explicit minimal permissions
example.code:
permissions: contents: read # default safe baseline
jobs: docker: permissions: contents: read packages: write # only this job needs GHCR push

rule.statement: Use OIDC (id-token: write permission + aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials with role-to-assume) instead of storing AWS/GCP/Azure credentials as repository secrets.
rule.rationale: OIDC tokens are short-lived (15 minutes), scoped to the specific workflow run, and require no rotation. Stored access keys are long-lived and rotate-or-breach scenarios are common.

example.title: OIDC AWS configuration
example.code:
permissions: id-token: write contents: read
steps: - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 with: role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/GitHubActionsRole aws-region: us-east-1

rule.statement: Set persist-credentials: false on actions/checkout unless subsequent steps explicitly need the token.
rule.rationale: Default checkout persists the GITHUB_TOKEN in the git config. Any later script that runs git commands can exfiltrate the token.

rule.statement: Pass GitHub event context to shell commands via environment variables, not inline expression interpolation.
rule.rationale: Inline ${{ github.event.pull_request.title }} in a run: step is interpreted before the shell executes, enabling command injection if the value contains shell metacharacters.

rule.statement: Never use pull_request_target with code from the fork without explicit trust verification.
rule.rationale: pull_request_target runs with write permissions and access to secrets. Checking out fork code in this context lets untrusted PRs exfiltrate all secrets.

rule.statement: Set concurrency: group on every workflow with cancel-in-progress: true for pull request triggers.
rule.rationale: Without concurrency control, each push to a PR branch starts a new workflow while the previous is still running, wasting runner minutes and producing confusing status updates.

example.title: Concurrency group configuration
example.note: Do not cancel-in-progress on main/release branches — a cancelled deploy halfway is worse than running both.

rule.statement: Use actions/cache with a lockfile hash as the primary key and a prefix-only restore-key for fallback.
rule.rationale: Exact-match keys ensure cache validity. Restore-keys allow partial hits that are still faster than a cold install.

example.title: Cache with lockfile hash key
example.code: - uses: actions/cache@v4 with: path: ~/.npm key: ${{ runner.os }}-npm-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }} restore-keys: | ${{ runner.os }}-npm-

rule.statement: Set timeout-minutes on every job; the default 360 minutes (6 hours) will silently block runners.
rule.rationale: Hung processes, network waits, or test deadlocks can hold a runner for hours. A 15-30 minute timeout forces investigation.

rule.statement: Use strategy.matrix for multi-version or multi-OS tests; set fail-fast: false unless all combinations must pass together.
rule.rationale: fail-fast: true (default) cancels all matrix jobs on the first failure, hiding whether the failure is version-specific. false reveals the full compatibility picture.

example.title: Matrix build with OS and Node versions
example.code: strategy: fail-fast: false matrix: os: [ubuntu-latest, macos-latest] node: [18, 20, 22] exclude: - os: macos-latest node: 18 include: - os: ubuntu-latest node: 20 coverage: true

rule.statement: Extract repeated deploy/test patterns into reusable workflows (workflow_call) rather than copy-pasting across repositories.
rule.rationale: Copied YAML diverges over time. Reusable workflows allow centralized security and logic fixes that propagate to all callers.

rule.statement: Use composite actions for repeated step sequences within the same repository; use reusable workflows for cross-repository sharing.
rule.rationale: Composite actions cannot use jobs/services; they are step bundles. Reusable workflows are full job definitions and support services, outputs, and secrets.

example.title: Reusable workflow invocation
example.code: # Caller workflow jobs: deploy-staging: uses: ./.github/workflows/deploy-reusable.yml with: environment: staging url: https://staging.example.com secrets: inherit

rule.statement: Set retention-days explicitly on all upload-artifact steps; default is 90 days which wastes storage.
rule.rationale: CI intermediates (build outputs, test reports) are only useful for the current PR lifecycle. 7 days is typical; only release assets need longer retention.

antipattern.description: Uploading node_modules/ as an artifact between jobs.
antipattern.reason: node_modules can be hundreds of MB. Use actions/cache for dependencies; use artifacts only for build outputs (dist/, coverage/).

template.purpose: Python CI with ruff/mypy and pytest coverage
LLM prompt for generating a workflow
LLM prompt for security-auditing a workflow
